summary: DHH argues that Automattic's demand for 8% of WP Engine's revenues fundamentally violates open source principles and the GPL license. He contends that open source's success depends on the clarity and certainty of its licensing regime, where obligations are spelled out in the license and nothing more. Despite having tribal reasons to side with WordPress, DHH prioritizes the integrity of open source licensing over personal allegiances. He draws a parallel to Rails, noting that many billion-dollar companies built on it contribute nothing back, and that's their right under the license. He concludes that Automattic wants the prestige of open source while extracting payments like a proprietary vendor.
key_points: Open source licensing derives its power from clarity and certainty — there cannot be shadow obligations beyond what the license states
Automattic's revenue demand against WP Engine sets a dangerous precedent that extends far beyond WordPress to all open source projects
DHH acknowledges the frustration of seeing companies profit enormously from your open source work without contributing back, but insists that's the deal
Automattic's trademark enforcement is especially hypocritical given they were part owners of WP Engine until 2018
If Automattic wants different terms, they can change their license going forward, but they can't retroactively impose obligations on GPL-licensed code
money_quotes: Automattic is completely out of line, and the potential damage to the open source world extends far beyond the WordPress.
A key part of why open source has been so successful over the last several decades is the clarity and certainty of its licensing regime.

None of the major licenses, however, say anything close to 'it's free but only until the project owners deem you too successful and then you'll have to pay 8% of your revenues to support the project'.
That's a completely bonkers and arbitrary standard based in the rule of spite, not law.
I don't even have a dog in this fight, only a set of principles.
That's the deal. That's open source. I give you a gift of code, you accept the terms of the license.
There cannot be a second set of shadow obligations that might suddenly apply, if you strike it rich using the software.
They want to retain WordPress' shine of open source, but also be able to extract their pound of flesh from any competitor that might appear, whenever they see fit. Screw that.
key_insight: Open source licenses must be the complete and final word on obligations — allowing project owners to impose retroactive revenue demands on successful users destroys the clarity and trust that made open source viable.
